content.headline: Most home-cooked food is pale and steamed instead of seared because the pan was never hot enough before the food went in.
points: Heat the pan empty for 1–3 minutes before adding oil — cast iron needs the most time, nonstick the least
Wait until the oil shimmers and moves like water across the surface — that's your green light
The first sizzle when food hits the pan tells you everything: loud and assertive means hot, quiet and wet means cold
Adjust heat in small steps and wait 30–45 seconds before adjusting again — burners lag behind the dial
content.tell_me_more: There's a physics reason for the shimmer test: when oil is cold, its viscosity holds it in place. As it heats past about 375°F, surface tension drops and it starts to move like a thin liquid. That shimmering, fluid motion is the visual signal that the oil is hot enough for browning reactions to happen the moment food touches it. If you add food before the shimmer, the first thing that happens is the food's surface moisture turns to steam — and steam is the enemy of a crust.

missions.title: The Shimmering Oil Test
missions.description: Heat an empty pan, test with water drops, add oil, and fry one egg using only the heat cues from this lesson — no guessing, just reading what the pan tells you.
missions.why_it_matters: A single egg is the perfect test: a properly heated pan means it sets immediately with lacy, crispy edges and releases cleanly.
missions.prompt: Show the finished fried egg still in the pan or just plated. Look for the edges: are they set, lacy, and slightly golden, or did they spread out flat and steam-cook? The whites should be fully set with no translucent patches.
reflection_choices: Egg sizzled on contact, edges went lacy and golden, released cleanly from the pan
Egg sizzled but edges didn't brown much — pan was hot but maybe not quite hot enough
Egg spread wide and cooked slowly — pan wasn't hot enough when it went in
Egg stuck to the pan or edges burned before the whites were fully set

missions.title: Reading Before and After
missions.description: Think about what the oil actually looked like right before you added the egg, and what happened in the first few seconds of contact.
missions.why_it_matters: Connecting what you saw to what happened builds the intuitive feedback loop that makes heat control automatic over time.
missions.prompt: Describe what the oil looked like in the pan right before you added the egg — was it shimmering, still, or smoking? And what happened in the first 5 seconds after the egg hit the pan? Be specific about sound, movement, and appearance.
